title: Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply
subtitle: Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.
caption:
Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply, 2022
Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between daily protein supply in grams per day per capita on the horizontal axis and the percentage of adults with obesity on the vertical axis for countries in 2022. The data points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A positive correlation is visible across the distribution, where nations with higher protein availability generally show higher obesity prevalence. At the upper extreme of the chart, Nauru and Tonga in Oceania display the highest obesity rates, both exceeding 70%, while maintaining protein supplies between 110 and 130 grams. The United States and Chile also appear in the upper-right quadrant, with obesity rates near 40% and protein supplies exceeding 120 grams. Conversely, the lower-left section of the chart contains numerous African nations such as Burundi, Somalia, and Rwanda, which record obesity rates below 10% alongside protein supplies under 50 grams. Asian countries like Vietnam, Nepal, and Bangladesh cluster in the lower-middle range, showing obesity rates under 10% with protein supplies between 60 and 80 grams. European nations including Iceland, Ireland, and Denmark occupy the far right of the horizontal axis with protein supplies reaching 140 grams, yet their obesity rates remain moderate, generally falling between 20% and 30%. The global average point sits near 100 grams of protein and roughly 18% obesity. The visible pattern shows that while low protein supply coincides with low obesity, high protein supply does not guarantee the highest obesity rates, as seen in the separation between the European cluster and the Pacific Island nations.
